commands.review_summary:
The PR adds pressure-aware backoff to memory-core cron-triggered dreaming sweeps, treats queued managed heartbeat dreaming as cron work, and updates focused tests and docs for the new scheduler behavior.

PR surface: Source +92, Tests +365, Docs +2. Total +459 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main loops through every resolved dreaming workspace for cron-triggered promotion without a pressure guard, and the linked reports include redacted logs showing 10 workspaces and high memory pressure. I did not run a live multi-workspace gateway in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: The remaining action is maintainer review of proof and scheduler compatibility, not a narrow mechanical repair that ClawSweeper should attempt automatically.

target.title: fix(memory-core): bound managed dreaming under pressure
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: managed memory dreaming cron could sweep every configured workspace without pausing, including when the gateway process was already under memory pressure.
- Solution: preserve the existing full scheduled sweep, but add a bounded memory-pressure backoff before each cron workspace attempt.
- Exact behavior now: each cron-triggered managed dreaming run still iterates every configured workspace in the same cron invocation. Before starting each workspace, memory-core checks process RSS and heap usage. If RSS is at least 1536 MiB or heap used is at least 1024 MiB, it waits and retries with exponential backoff: 30s, 60s, 120s, 240s, then capped at 300s between retries. It keeps retrying at the capped delay until pressure clears or the enclosing cron run stops.
- What did NOT change: direct non-cron dreaming requests are not delayed by this scheduler guard; the dreaming frequency/config switches are unchanged; memory storage formats are unchanged; cron still owns one scheduled managed dreaming job.

## Motivation

- Observed gateway performance logs showed managed memory dreaming processing 10 workspaces in one background tick, taking 69621 ms, while nearby diagnostics reported high RSS and saturated event-loop/CPU conditions.
- The desired fix is to avoid adding more work while the process is under pressure, without dropping the existing full-sweep semantics for multi-workspace users.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84847
- Closes #84849
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Managed memory-core dreaming cron keeps the existing all-workspace sweep, but waits under gateway RSS/heap pressure before starting the next workspace instead of continuing immediately.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw worktree with focused memory-core Vitest coverage for the managed cron seam; before evidence comes from redacted runtime diagnostics captured from the affected gateway.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-cache-dreaming-final3 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose --testTimeout=30000`; `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`; `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm docs:check-mdx`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```shell
$ OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-cache-dreaming-final3 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose --testTimeout=30000
1 file passed, 51 tests passed

$ pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled
# passed

$ pnpm docs:list
# passed

$ pnpm docs:check-mdx
# passed

$ git diff --check
# no output
```

- Observed result after fix: The regression tests verify that managed cron dreaming remains a full workspace sweep when pressure is low, backs off between cron workspaces and then continues when pressure clears, keeps retrying at the 5 minute cap until the next workspace can run, and treats queued managed heartbeat dreaming events as cron work for the pressure guard.
- What was not tested: A live multi-workspace gateway run with private agent/session data was not rerun; the fix is verified at the memory-core cron seam with redacted before evidence and focused tests. The broader checkpoint artifact contributor is covered separately by #84824.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```shell
memory-core: dreaming promotion complete (workspaces=10, candidates=10, applied=1, failed=0)
observed managed background task duration: 69621ms
memory pressure warning: rssBytes=1827979264 heapUsedBytes=684072312 thresholdBytes=1610612736
liveness warning: eventLoopUtilization=0.991 cpuCoreRatio=1.107 active=0 waiting=0 queued=0
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Managed cron dreaming collected every configured workspace and processed them in one cron delivery without pausing between workspaces when process memory was already above warning thresholds.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered short-term dreaming promotion but did not exercise multi-workspace managed cron sweeps, pressure backoff between workspaces, capped retry behavior, or queued managed heartbeat cron delivery.
- Contributing context (if known): The managed heartbeat path can deliver the cron system event through the before-agent-reply hook, so it needs to enter the same cron pressure path as a direct cron trigger.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: managed cron dreaming keeps the full configured workspace sweep, waits under RSS/heap pressure before starting a workspace, doubles retry delay from 30 seconds to a 5 minute cap, continues in the same cron invocation after pressure clears, and treats queued managed heartbeat dreaming events as cron work for pressure checks.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is owned inside the memory-core dreaming cron seam and can be exercised without private gateway sessions or provider credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing dreaming tests covered promotion behavior but not the pressure and backoff cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Scheduled memory dreaming still uses `dreaming.frequency` as the cadence for a full configured-workspace sweep. When gateway RSS or heap usage is above the warning threshold before a workspace starts, scheduled dreaming waits before continuing. The wait starts at 30 seconds, doubles, and caps at 5 minutes between workspace attempts. No remaining workspaces are intentionally deferred to a later cron tick by memory-core; the run continues when pressure clears unless the enclosing cron run stops.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
cron tick -> workspace A -> workspace B -> workspace C -> ... without pressure pause

After:
cron tick -> workspace A -> pressure check before B -> 30s/60s/120s/240s/300s capped wait while pressured -> workspace B -> pressure check before C -> ... full sweep continues
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux worktree
- Runtime/container: Node via repo test wrapper
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): memory-core plugin cron seam
- Relevant config (redacted): memory-core dreaming enabled with multiple agent workspaces

### Steps

1. Run the focused memory-core dreaming tests.
2. Exercise cron-triggered dreaming with multiple workspaces and pressure-mocked process memory.
3. Confirm all workspaces are still swept, pressure waits happen before workspace attempts, backoff caps at 5 minutes, and queued heartbeat delivery enters the cron pressure path.

### Expected

- Cron-triggered managed dreaming preserves the full configured workspace sweep and waits under RSS/heap pressure before starting the next workspace.

### Actual

- After the patch, focused tests pass for full-sweep behavior, pressure backoff, capped retries, and queued heartbeat delivery.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused memory-core dreaming Vitest coverage, bundled plugin lint, docs list/check-mdx, and diff hygiene.
- Edge cases checked: queued managed heartbeat delivery under pressure, low-pressure full sweep, multi-step pressure backoff between workspaces, and capped retry behavior at 5 minutes.
- What you did **not** verify: private live multi-workspace gateway run.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If memory pressure remains high for a long time, a scheduled dreaming cron run may take longer because it waits before starting the next workspace.
  - Mitigation: The backoff is capped at 5 minutes, logs each wait with RSS/heap context, and preserves the existing full-sweep behavior instead of dropping or skipping workspaces.
target.author: galiniliev
